chunk_summary: Partial enteral nutrition (PEN) and food-based exclusion diets can support growth and maintain remission in pediatric IBD when exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) is not feasible. Structured plans with clear targets are essential to avoid nutritional deficiencies and ensure treatment efficacy.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of partial enteral nutrition (PEN) in pediatric IBD management.
Identify the benefits and limitations of food-based exclusion diets for maintaining remission in Crohn’s disease.
Learn how to set and monitor nutritional targets to ensure adequate growth and inflammation control.
Recognize the importance of reassessment and escalation of therapy when dietary interventions fail.
key_facts: PEN combines a defined proportion of calories from nutritionally complete formulas with selected foods.
Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet (CDED) with PEN has trial evidence for symptom and biomarker improvement in children.
Calorie targets for formula should be set as a fixed percentage of estimated needs and reassessed within weeks.
Markers like albumin, CRP, and fecal calprotectin should guide dietary adjustments and therapy escalation.
Short-term reduction of insoluble fiber and lactose may improve tolerance during flares but should not be prolonged.
In resource-limited settings, ensure affordability and continuity of formulas or provide energy-dense substitutes with micronutrient supplementation.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet combined with PEN has trial evidence for symptom and biomarker improvement in some children and can be useful when full EEN adherence is unrealistic.
document_key_topics: Partial enteral nutrition (PEN) in pediatric IBD
Food-based exclusion diets for Crohn’s disease
Nutritional targets and reassessment in IBD management
Micronutrient risks and dietary adjustments during flares
